question: Comparing Figures 1.2-5 and 5.1-1, what internal components of the NPM are visible in the cutaway views?

nuscale_ch01_0063.title: Emergency Core Cooling System Valve Design Certification Application Demonstration Test
nuscale_ch01_0063.summary: This section describes the demonstration test program for the Emergency Core Cooling System (ECCS) valves in the NuScale reactor design. The program aimed to verify the functional performance of ECCS valves—including their operation at reactor pressures and temperatures, and in borated coolant—using test setups that replicated actual valve configurations and features such as the inadvertent actuation block (IAB). Testing was conducted at a specialized facility to ensure the valves met safety and operational requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain fuel integrity, minimize solid deposits, and ensure uniform boron concentration under all operating conditions, supporting both performance and safety requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0032.title: Radioactive Waste Management System
nuscale_ch01_0032.summary: The section describes the radioactive waste management system at the NuScale Power Plant, noting that liquid, gaseous, and solid waste systems are detailed in Chapter 11, along with effluent monitoring. It also outlines the general arrangement of major plant structures, focusing on the Reactor Building (RXB), which is a reinforced, seismically designed concrete structure housing the nuclear power modules and providing radiation protection for personnel.

nuscale_ch01_0802.title: SRP 15.8, Rev 2: Anticipated Transients without ScramII.2 Acceptance Criteria for Pressurized Water Reactors (PWRs)
nuscale_ch01_0802.summary: The section explains that the NuScale reactor design uses a single-failure-proof crane system, in compliance with NUREG-0612 guidelines, ensuring that no credible single component failure can result in dropping a critical load such as a spent fuel cask. Because of this robust design, accident analyses for spent fuel cask or NPM drop events, including radiological consequence assessments, are not required or performed.

nuscale_ch01_0033.title: General Arrangement of Major Structures and Equipment
nuscale_ch01_0033.summary: This section describes the general arrangement of major structures and equipment at a NuScale Power Plant, focusing on the central placement and design of the Reactor Building (RXB). The RXB is a large, reinforced concrete structure designed for safety and radiation protection, housing the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) in a common reactor pool with individual bays arranged in two rows. The section also references detailed figures and notes that some buildings, like the administration and training building, are outside the scope of the report.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes key components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. It removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining pressure boundary integrity to prevent radionuclide release. The RCS interfaces with several other systems, including emergency cooling and chemical control, to ensure safe and efficient operation.

nuscale_ch01_0002.title: Introduction
nuscale_ch01_0002.summary: The Introduction section outlines that this document is the Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) for the NuScale small modular reactor, submitted as part of the standard design certification process under 10 CFR 52. It describes the NuScale Power Module’s design, safety features, and scalability, highlighting key innovations such as passive safety systems, compact design, and advanced digital controls. The FSAR covers both individual module details and multi-module plant configurations.

nuscale_ch05_0005.title: System Components
nuscale_ch05_0005.summary: This section describes the main components of the NuScale reactor system, focusing on the Reactor Pressure Vessel (RPV), Reactor Coolant System (RCS) piping, and the Pressurizer (PZR). The RPV serves as a primary barrier to fission product release and houses key reactor internals, while the RCS piping connects various subsystems and the PZR is integrated into the RPV to maintain system pressure. Each component's functions and connections within the overall reactor system are briefly outlined.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes special plant features of the NuScale Power Plant, including the Central Utility Building (CUB) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs), which house essential but nonsafety-related systems and backup equipment. It also highlights human factors engineering (HFE) considerations, emphasizing fail-safe design, automation, digital controls, and workload management to minimize human error and meet regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0015.title: General Plant Description
nuscale_ch01_0015.summary: This section provides a summary of the NuScale plant’s overall design, including key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, cooling water, and waste management. It explains the distinction between certified and site-specific design elements, clarifies the use of representative information for context, and notes how security-related details are handled. Each COL applicant must reference the NuScale FSAR and provide site-specific information as required.

nuscale_ch01_0110.title: Figure 1.7-1b: Electrical Symbols
nuscale_ch01_0110.summary: This section provides a comprehensive legend of electrical symbols and abbreviations used in NuScale’s Final Safety Analysis Report drawings, including representations for switches, relays, indicators, and logic gates. It also explains the meaning of various indicator light colors, logic gate functions, and common abbreviations relevant to electrical and control systems.

nuscale_ch01_0111.title: Figure 1.7-2: Instrumentatio n and Controls Symbol Legend
nuscale_ch01_0111.summary: This section provides a legend explaining the symbols used for instrumentation and controls in the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report. It defines the logic gate functions (AND, OR, NOT), bistable outputs, control room indicators, switches, and other control functions, as well as notes on piping and instrumentation diagram conventions.

nuscale_ch01_0068.title: Electrical and Instrumentation and Control Drawings
nuscale_ch01_0068.summary: This section explains that the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report includes simplified electrical, instrumentation, and control (I&C) drawings to visually clarify design information alongside the written text. It references tables and figures listing and explaining the symbols used in these diagrams, and notes that applicants referencing the NuScale design must provide additional site-specific diagrams and legends as needed.

nuscale_ch01_0806.title: SRP 15.8, Rev 2: Anticipated Transients without ScramII.3.C Analysis Demonstrating the Failure Probability of Failing the ATWS Success Criteria is Sufficiently Small
nuscale_ch01_0806.summary: The section summarizes NuScale’s approach to meeting regulatory criteria for Anticipated Transients Without Scram (ATWS) events. NuScale relies on internal diversity within its Module Protection System (MPS) and Reactor Protection System (RPS) to reduce ATWS risk, partially conforming to requirements for a diverse scram system and demonstrating a sufficiently low probability of ATWS failure. Certain equipment requirements do not apply to the NuScale design, and the approach is considered a simpler method to meet the intent of the ATWS Rule.

nuscale_ch01_0807.title: DSRS 15.9.A, Rev 0: Thermal Hydraulic Stability Review ResponsibilitiesII.1 No requirements - null.
nuscale_ch01_0807.summary: This section reviews NuScale’s conformance with regulatory criteria for anticipated transients without scram (ATWS) and thermal hydraulic stability. The NuScale design uses internal diversity within its module protection system (MPS) to address ATWS risks, partially meeting some regulatory requirements, while other requirements are not applicable or fully met due to the unique design features. For thermal hydraulic stability, the design meets the requirements of GDC 12, with no additional requirements specified in some areas.

edges.description: Section A specifies the locations and functions of ECCS valves and related internal components, while Section B provides a general summary of the reactor coolant system and its internal structures, allowing identification of which internal components are visible in the referenced figures.

Section A discusses the design and testing of internal NPM components (ECCS valves, control rod drive shafts), while Section B references figures (including Figure 1.2-2) that present the general arrangement and layout of major plant structures and equipment, thus both sections provide information about the visibility and location of internal NPM components in cutaway views.

Section A describes specific internal components of the NPM, such as ECCS valves and control rod drive shafts, whose locations and visibility can be cross-checked in the general arrangement and cutaway views referenced in Section B.

Section A identifies the location and function of the reactor vent valves (RVVs) as part of the ECCS, while Section B describes the RVVs' operational role in both normal and emergency conditions, connecting their physical presence in the NPM cutaway views to their described functions.

Section A describes the internal components of the NPM visible in cutaway views, while Section B references general plant layout figures that may include the NPM but does not detail its internal components, so Section A provides specific details that Section B's general description lacks.

Section A describes the internal components of the NPM, such as the reactor coolant system and its subsystems, while Section B references figures that show the general arrangement of major structures and equipment, thus Section A provides specific details that can be visually identified in the cutaway views referenced in Section B.

Section A provides a general overview of the main internal components of the NPM visible in cutaway views, while Section B specifies additional system features and functions related to those components, such as degasification and pressure boundary integrity.

Section A and Section B are identical excerpts that both reference the detailed discussion of the radioactive waste management system and the general arrangement of major structures and equipment, including references to figures and chapters for further detail.

Both Section A and Section B reference Figures 1.2-5 and 5.1-1, which provide cutaway views of the NPM, allowing identification of internal components by visually comparing the figures as described in their respective sections.

Section B provides a detailed description of internal NPM components and their functions, which are visually represented in the cutaway views compared in the analysis question, while Section A does not address internal component visibility.

Section A provides a general layout and references Figure 1.2-2 for the arrangement of major structures, while Section B describes specific internal components and functions of the reactor coolant system, both contributing information about which internal NPM components are depicted in the referenced cutaway figures.

Section A provides detailed drawings and visual clarifications of internal components, which can be used as evidence to support Section B's claims about the design's conformance with regulatory criteria for safety analysis.

Section A and Section B both discuss conformance with SRP 15.8 requirements for ATWS events in the NuScale FSAR, referencing the same regulatory criteria and design approach regarding the module protection system (MPS).
